coredata.dc:title: A paper-based microfluidic sensor array combining molecular imprinting technology and carbon quantum dots for the discrimination of nitrophenol isomers
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Liang
Mei, Xuecui
Peng, Zhengchun
Yang, Jiao
Li, Yingchun
coredata.dc:description: Paper-based microfluidic analytical devices (μPADs) have recently attracted attention as a rapid test kit owing to their low cost and nonrequirement for external driving pump. However, low accuracy and poor anti-interference ability of μPADs under complex detection condition limit their practical applications. Here, we present a facile way to prepare a novel fluorescence sensor-array μPAD for multi-analyte discrimination based on molecular imprinting technology, and its sensing behavior was studied by using three nitrophenol (NP) isomers (2-, 3-, and 4-NP) as the testing models. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) emitting blue light were grafted on glass-fiber paper, followed by in-situ modification of three types of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) with 2-, 3-, and 4-NP as template. Each sensing unit on the array showed differential yet cross-reactive binding affinity to NP isomers, resulting in distinct fluorescence quenching efficiency. Thus, precise distinguishment of the three NPs was realized with the MIPs/CQDs/paper-based sensor array. Furthermore, the discrimination ability of the platform was evaluated in mixtures of the NP isomers. Practicability of this apparatus was validated by identification of blind samples and 100% accuracy was achieved. The μPAD has proven to be highly sensitive and accurate, which will serve as an ideal analytical tool in the fields of environment monitoring, disease prognosis, food safety and so on.
coredata.dcterms:subject: μPADs
Sensor array
Carbon quantum dots
MIPs
Nitrophenol isomers

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection and identification of NP isomers
Analysis of blind and real water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shenzhen City

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work financially supported by Shenzhen Science and Technology Program ( KQTD20170810105439418 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 82104121 , 81973280 , 82173778 ), and Key Program of Natural Science Foundation of Shenzhen ( JCYJ20200109113410174 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CQDs. Scale bar is 50 nm. SEM images of (B) bare paper, (C) CQDs/Paper, (D) 2-NP MIPs/CQDs/Paper, (E) 3-NP MIPs/CQDs/Paper and (F) 4-NP MIPs/CQDs/Paper. Scale bars are 10 µm.

(A) FTIR spectra of CQDs, CQDs/Paper and 4-NP MIPs/CQDs/Paper. (B) The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs. The fluorescence emission spectra of (C) 2-NP, (D) 3-NP and (E) 4-NP MIPs/CQDs/Paper in NP solution. Their corresponding Stern–Volmer plots were showed in the inset pictures. Error bars represent the standard deviation from three parallel tests.

(A) Fluorescence change generated by the three NPs and platform. The canonical score plots for the discrimination of the three NPs at (B)10 μM and (D) 5 μM were analyzed by LDA. HCA plot for the discrimination of the three NPs at (C) 10 μM and (E) 5 μM.

(A) Canonical score plots for the discrimination of 2-NP/3-NP mixture at 10 μM were analyzed by LDA. (B) HCA plot for the discrimination of 2-NP/3-NP mixture at 10 μM. (C) The canonical score plots for the discrimination of three NP isomers (10 µM) and some interferents (50 µM). (D) The canonical score plots for the discrimination of three NP isomers (10 µM) in river water.
Schematic illustration of the fabrication process of the fluorescence sensor-array platform for detection and discrimination of NP isomers.

head.ce:title: A paper-based microfluidic sensor array combining molecular imprinting technology and carbon quantum dots for the discrimination of nitrophenol isomers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, Harbin Institute of Technology, Shenzhen 518055, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Science, Harbin Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of chemistry and chemical engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Optoelectronic Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Optoelectronic Engineering, Shenzhen University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Optoelectronic Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, 518060, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Science, Harbin Institute of Technology, Shenzhen 518055, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Science, Harbin Institute of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Paper-based microfluidic analytical devices (μPADs) have recently attracted attention as a rapid test kit owing to their low cost and nonrequirement for external driving pump. However, low accuracy and poor anti-interference ability of μPADs under complex detection condition limit their practical applications. Here, we present a facile way to prepare a novel fluorescence sensor-array μPAD for multi-analyte discrimination based on molecular imprinting technology, and its sensing behavior was studied by using three nitrophenol (NP) isomers (2-, 3-, and 4-NP) as the testing models. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) emitting blue light were grafted on glass-fiber paper, followed by in-situ modification of three types of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) with 2-, 3-, and 4-NP as template. Each sensing unit on the array showed differential yet cross-reactive binding affinity to NP isomers, resulting in distinct fluorescence quenching efficiency. Thus, precise distinguishment of the three NPs was realized with the MIPs/CQDs/paper-based sensor array. Furthermore, the discrimination ability of the platform was evaluated in mixtures of the NP isomers. Practicability of this apparatus was validated by identification of blind samples and 100% accuracy was achieved. The μPAD has proven to be highly sensitive and accurate, which will serve as an ideal analytical tool in the fields of environment monitoring, disease prognosis, food safety and so on.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrophenols (NPs), as important industrial raw materials, are broadly used in various fields (Xiong et al., 2019). Due to their difficulty in degradability, NPs have caused serious pollution to the environment and been classified as priority toxic pollutants and hazardous wastes in many countries (Lu et al., 2019; Wang and Wu, 2006). There are three isomers in the NP family, i.e., 2-, 3- and 4-NP. The toxicity of 4-NP is much higher than 2- and 3-NPs (Chi et al., 2013). In addition, 2-NP and 4-NP as the hydrolysis products of pesticides, widely exist in wastewater and food, imposing adverse effects on metabolism of organisms (Guo and Guo, 2013). However, they are often present in the environment in the form of mixtures (Xu et al., 2011; Zhang et al., 2015). Thus, identification of the three NP isomers in a simple and sensitive approach is essential for protection of environment and human health.

Paper-based microfluidic analytical devices (μPADs) have attracted great attention because of their low cost and ease of use (Cate et al., 2015). They utilize capillary force to deliver samples without the need of an external driving pump, which have played a role in medical diagnosis (Nie et al., 2010; Rossini et al., 2018), environmental monitoring (Kung et al., 2019; Xie et al., 2019), biochemical analysis (Hao et al., 2021; Jiang et al., 2019), food security (Trofimchuk et al., 2020) and other fields. Till now, μPADs have been integrated with various detection techniques such as colorimetry (Sriram et al., 2017), electrochemistry (Pradela-Filho et al., 2020), fluorescence (Liu et al., 2019), chemiluminescence (Hassanzadeh et al., 2019), etc. Among them, fluorescence-based μPADs are gradually becoming a new development trend with the merits of portability, integration, and sensitivity (Yamada et al., 2015).

Generally, fluorescence analysis is mainly achieved by combining fluorescent materials with NP recognition receptors. A wide variety of fluorescent materials such as organic fluorophores (Ponnuvel et al., 2016), quantum dots (Ju and Chen, 2014) and metal nanoclusters (Bai et al., 2020) have been developed to construct fluorescent sensors. Among them, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as zero-dimensional nanomaterials with sizes ranging from 1 nm to 10 nm, are ideal fluorescent sensing materials because of their simple preparation, stable fluorescence, low toxicity and good biocompatibility (Gu et al., 2022). Compared with natural receptors (i.e., antibody, enzyme, and aptamer), molecular imprinting polymers (MIPs) as a kind of bionic material derived from the perception of antibody-antigen specificity, possess obvious virtues such as mechanical and chemical stability, and low cost (Wu et al., 2020; Fu et al., 2022).

Although MIPs have specific recognition sites that are complementary to template molecules, there still exist cross-reactivity issues that MIPs respond to structural analogs of template (Lavignac et al., 2004). Fortunately, sensor arrays have been proved to be a simple yet effective tool for differentiating analytes with similar structures or properties by using cross reactivity (Li et al., 2019). Multiple sensing units in one array interact with multiple analytes to produce different responses. The obtained responses can be processed through linear discriminant analysis (LDA), hierarchical clustering analysis (HCA) or principal component analysis (PCA), offering a distinctive “fingerprint” that can discriminate diverse analytes (Lin et al., 2020; Ouyang et al., 2013; Wu et al., 2017). The specific recognition ability and cross-reactivity of MIPs provide an alternative strategy for construction of sensor array, and μPADs have been regarded as an ideal vessel for MIPs loading (Qi et al., 2018, 2017; Sun et al., 2014). The elaborate combination of the three has broad prospects in developing portable apparatus for high-throughput and multiple analytes detection in a cost-efficient way. To the best of our knowledge, there are no reports on sensor array systems based on MIPs-modified μPADs.

Herein, a paper-based sensor array using three types of MIPs/CQDs as sensing units for sensitive and accurate discrimination of NP isomers was developed. In each unit, CQDs were adopted to generate fluorescent signals, and the MIPs with 2-, 3- or 4-NP as the individual template owned different binding capacities to the three targets. On the basis of this sensor array, single and mixed NP isomers were well distinguished by analyzing the signal-off fluorescence response patterns with the assistance of LDA. Meanwhile, this platform demonstrated good recognition capability and performance in practical applications. As an inexpensive and portable device, it allows for on-site measurements in resource-limited areas.

Citric acid (CA), phenylalanine, sodium N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Tetramethoxysilane (TEOS), ammonia solution (NH3·H2O), 2-NP, 3-NP, 4-NP, phenol, hydroquinone, 2-metheylphenol, 2-aminophenol, 2-nitroaniline and 2-nitrochlorobenzene were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Co. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade and directly used without further purification. Whatman glass fiber paper (GF/A18205) were obtained from Shanghai Jinpan biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

Fluorescence tests were conducted with a spectrofluometer (Fluoromax-4, HORIBA, France). Morphological information was obtained with a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi S-4800, Japan) and a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-1200EX, Japan). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded with a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrometer (Japan) and a Thermo scientific Nicolet iS50 spectrometer (USA), respectively.

Preparation of CQDs was performed by hydrothermal method (Pu et al., 2020). Briefly, 1 mmol CA and 1 mmol phenylalanine were dissolved in 10 mL H2O, and then the mixture was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. The reaction solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove the solid. The supernatant was dialyzed against double distilled water through a 500 Da molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) membrane for 48 h. Finally, the dialysis solution was freeze-dried to obtained CQDs.

The paper chips were prepared according to the method reported in the literature (Li et al., 2017) with some modification. Firstly, glass fiber paper was cropped into 5 mm × 5 mm and immersed in 0.2 M HCl solution for 30 min and washed with deionized water to remove excess HCl. The paper squares were immersed in 20 mL of ethanol/water (1:1, v/v) containing 200 μL APTES for 4 h. After that, they were anchored on a petri dish to prevent floating. CQDs (10 mg), 5 mL NHS (50 mg/mL) and 5 mL EDC (25 mg/mL) were added and reacted with the paper in the dark for 6 h. The obtained paper was denoted as CQDs/Paper. Subsequently, three kinds of MIPs with 2-, 3- and 4-NP as the respective template were separately synthesized on CQDs/Paper. Briefly, functional monomer (APTES) and template molecules (2-, 3- or 4-NP) were mixed in 10 mL ethanol and stirred at 200 rpm for 30 min. Then cross-linking agent (TEOS) and initiator (NH3·H2O) were added. When the solution became turbid, the paper was immersed in and left overnight under stirring (120 rpm). Finally, the MIPs/CQDs/Paper were obtained by elution with ethanol/acetonitrile (8:2, v/v) to remove the template molecules. The amount of functional monomer, template molecules, crosslinking agent and initiator were 100 μL, 10 mg, 100 μL, and 100 μL, respectively. The same process was used for the synthesis of non-imprinted polymers (NIPs) except that no template molecules were added (NIPs/CQDs/Paper). All paper chips were stored at 4 °C when not in use.

The μPAD was designed using Solid-Works 2014 3D CAD software (Concord, MA, U.S.A.) and then printed on fiber paper by spraying wax. The μPAD was composed of three layers. The bottom one was loaded with three paper chips modified with MIPs/CQDs. The middle layer was printed with one sample inlet, channels and three detection cells. The top layer contained four cutouts corresponding to the sample inlet and the detection cells in the middle layer. Finally, the three layers were assembled and fixed to complete the construction of the μPAD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection and identification of NP isomers
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 150 μL NP solution at different concentrations (1–100 μM) was dropped into the inlet of μPAD. Subsequently, the solution flowed to detection cells by capillary force and reacted with MIPs on the paper chip. Finally, response signals of the paper chip were recorded by a spectrofluometer with the excitation wavelength of 360 nm and slit widths of 5 nm. The fluorescence response was determined from (F 0 −F)/F, where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the μPAD in the absence and presence of the NP isomers, respectively. For identification of NP isomers (5 μM and 10 μM), the fluorescence signals of the μPAD in the three NP isomers solution were recorded, and the whole experiment was repeated five times to obtain a 3 × 3 × 5 training data matrix (3 MIPs×3 NPs×5 replicates). Finally, LDA and HCA were performed by origin 2019b. The mixtures of NP isomers were tested using the similar approach with the total concentration of 10 μM. The presented μPAD sensor was adopted to detect real water samples from the tap water, Xili Lake and Dashahe River (Shenzhen, China). Before analysis, simple filtering pretreatment was conducted with a filter membrane (0.22 µm), and the rest procedure followed the above-mentioned steps.

As a proof of concept, we chose NP isomers as the analysis model. CQDs dispersed in solvent feature the property of easy aggregation during long-term storage, resulting in a certain degree of fluorescence quenching. To stabilize their fluorescence, CQDs was immobilized onto paper substrates. The modification process of the μPAD was shown in Fig. 1. First, paper chips decorated with CQDs and MIPs were prepared. Before modification, the bare glass-fiber paper was soaked in HCl solution to remove impurities and activate hydroxyl groups on the paper surface, and APTES was used to introduce amino groups. Then, CQDs with a large number of carboxyl functional groups were grafted via covalent binding under activation of EDS/NHS. After that, three MIPs with 2-, 3- and 4-NP as one of the template molecules were polymerized on CQDs/Paper by a facile sol-gel method, leading to decrement in fluorescence. After removal of the template molecules, imprinting cavities were generated and fluorescence was restored. The cavities in MIPs can specifically recognize and bind templates, resulting in fluorescence quenching of the paper chip.

Subsequently, the three paper chips with different MIPs were assembled into the μPAD, and the photo of the devices was illustrated in Fig. S1. When the sample was dropped into the sample inlet, it was transferred through capillary force into detection cells, in which different paper chips were equipped for fluorescence detection. It is worth mentioning that the numbers of detection cells can be adjusted according to actual needs, and the paper chips can be replaced flexibly ( Scheme 1).

In this paper, a hydrothermal method was utilized to synthesize CQDs. The TEM image in Fig. 1A shows that diameters of the prepared CQDs are about 4 nm. The surface morphologies of blank paper, CQDs/Paper and MIPs/CQDs/Paper were characterized by SEM. As shown in Fig. 1B, the paper matrix composed of fibers with uneven diameter. When the CQDs were modified on the paper surface, no obvious change was observed on the fiber due to the small size of the quantum dots (Fig. 1C). After deposition of MIPs (Fig. 1D-F), the fibers were wrapped with spherical polymer. The chemical structures of CQDs and paper chips were studied by FTIR spectroscopy. As displayed in Fig. 2A, CQDs have a strong –OH stretching vibration peak at 3416 cm−1 and a –CO stretching vibration peak at 1702 cm−1, indicating that the CQDs surface contains a lot of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. Since glass fiber contains a large amount of SiO2, the characteristic peaks of CQDs/Paper at 469 cm−1 and 780 cm−1 originate from the stretching vibration of the SiO bond, and the peak at 1060 cm−1 is generated by the antisymmetric stretching vibration of the SiOSi bond. The peaks located at 1612 cm−1and 1702 cm−1 are respectively ascribed to stretching vibration of –N–H and –CO, which implies successful grafting of CQDs on the paper surface. For MIPs/CQDs/Paper, enhancement of Si–O characteristic peak at 1060 cm−1 suggests the formation of polymers. The above results verified the successful preparation of MIPs/CQDs/Paper chips.

Optical properties of CQDs and paper chips were studied. In Fig. 2B, the UV–vis and fluorescence emission spectra show that CQDs have a wide excitation band in a range of 300–400 nm, and an emission peak is found at the wavelength of 440 nm upon exciting at 360 nm (Fig. 2B). Fig. S2 shows the fluorescence change during the paper chip preparation procedure. The bare paper chip had no fluorescence under an ultraviolet lamp, while it emitted blue light after CQDs modification, further confirming the successful decoration of CQDs. The introduction of MIPs led to significant decrease of fluorescence intensity. After removal of template molecules, the fluorescence recovered to 55% of the initial value, suggesting generation of imprinting cavities that allow light transmission.

To guarantee that the experiments were conducted under the optimal conditions, the key factors affecting the experiments were investigated. Firstly, the effects of the amount of functional monomer and crosslinking agent for MIP preparation were assessed. Generally, the thinner the MIP is, the fewer imprinting cavities are. However, thick film affects the complete elution of template molecules and obstructs the transmission of fluorescence. According to the fluorescence responses of 4-NP MIPs/CQDs/Paper, 100 μL of APTES and 100 μL TEOS were chosen as the optimal conditions for the synthesis of MIPs in the paper chip (Fig. S3).

The amount of CQDs on the paper chip was optimized. As shown in Fig. S4, at the same NP concentration, the value of F 0/F decreased with increasing the amount of CQDs and correspondingly the fluorescence quenching effect got weak. This is related to the fact that the higher the concentration of CQDs, the stronger the initial signal of fluorescence, while the ratio of F 0 to F decreased. Additionally, though the highest sensitivity was obtained at 5 mg CQDs, the response signals were liable to saturate as the concentration of 4-NP went up. When the amount of CQDs was more than 10 mg, the sensitivity of the paper chip declined. Therefore, 10 mg was chosen as the dosage of CQDs to modify the paper chip.

Then the time for eluting templates from polymers was optimized by detecting template molecules in eluent. As shown in Fig. S5, an absorption peak of the templates could be clearly measured within 4-min elution, which significantly diminished after 8-min elution. To ensure complete removal of the templates, elution time was set at 12 min.

The pH effect of NPs solution was also studied. In Fig. S6A, the response signals increased dramatically from pH 5.0–7.0, and then decreased significantly. Under acidic conditions, it is very likely that the surface of CQDs was occupied by a large number of carboxylic acid groups, hindering combination of electron-hole pair, which eventually leads to reduction in the signal output of MIPs/CQDs (Singh et al., 2020). Under alkaline conditions, the protonation of the amino groups at the recognition sites of MIPs/CQDs impaired the binding affinity towards the template molecules (Chullasat et al., 2018). Therefore, the pH of NPs solution was set at 7.0.

Next, adsorption time of the paper chips in NPs solution were investigated. As shown in Fig. S6B, the response signal enhanced dramatically within the initial 15 min and became stable thereafter. Therefore, 18 min was chosen as the adsorption equilibrium time.

The fluorescence spectra of the three NP isomers under the corresponding paper chips were studied. As illustrated in Fig. 2C-2E, the fluorescence intensity of the paper chips decreased continuously with the increase of NPs concentration and showed a good linear relationship in the range of 0–100 μM. The detection limits (S/N = 3) of the three paper chips were 0.347 μM, 0.303 μM and 0.388 μM for 2-, 3- and 4-NP, respectively. There are two possible reasons to explain the fluorescent quenching effect: 1) inner-filter effect, that is, UV absorption bands of the three NP isomers overlap with the excitation peak of CQDs; 2) MIP-induced gate effect, that is, the rebinding of imprinting cavities of MIPs with template molecules reduces fluorescence intensity. As a control, NIPs/CQDs/Paper chip was also employed to detect the three NPs, and their sensitivities were much lower than those obtained from MIPs/CQDs/Paper chips (Fig. S7). Imprinting factor (IF) is an important index to evaluate selectivity of MIPs (Kong et al., 2018). Herein, the IFs of the three MIPs were 4.4, 4.0 and 5.2, indicating that the MIPs possessed high binding affinity to the targets. The calculation formula of IF was given in Supporting Information.

Although MIPs provide specific recognition sites for target molecules, they also have affinity for structural analogs of template molecules. Such cross reactivity often imposes adverse effects on detection accuracy. To circumvent this issue, we designed a sensor array, which translated the response differences from multiple analytes into data information for identification. Since the selective difference and cross response between MIPs and different target molecules was the sensing principle of the sensor array, fluorescence signals of 2-, 3-, 4-NP on the μPAD were detected. As shown in Fig. 3A, all the fluorescence intensities declined, indicating that the prepared μPAD responded to all the three analytes. The responsiveness of the MIPs to the template molecules were significantly higher than other analytes. Therefore, the constructed μPAD can produce a unique response signal for each analyte, and it is feasible to use this device to discriminate the three NPs.

The μPAD was used for identification of NPs at the concentration of 10 μM. The μPAD preformed 5 replications of the NPs detection to obtain a 3 × 3 × 5 training data matrix (3 MIPs×3 NPs×5 replicates). Since LDA can identify linear combinations of features that distinguish between two or more types of events, it maximizes the variance ratio of events and achieves maximum data separation. LDA reduces the size of the training matrix and transforms it into typical factors. In Fig. 3B, typical patterns of the results were clustered entirely into three different groups, and each group contained five points representing the response of each analyte. These groups were visualized as a well-clustered two-dimensional plot with a total variance of 100%. Hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA), a statistical classification method based on Euclidean distance, was used to analyze the similarity of NPs. As displayed in Fig. 3C, all 15 cases (3 NPs with 5 replicates) were correctly assigned to their respective groups.

Furthermore, the three NPs at the concentration of 5 μM were analyzed in order to test sensitivity of the array. As shown in Fig S8A, the quenching effects of the three isomers were significantly different, and a well-grouped two-factor plot with 100% dispersion was obtained by LDA. As expected, all the 15 cases were correctly classified to their respective groups (Fig. S8B).

The platform was subsequently used to measure NPs mixtures at different molar ratios (total concentration of 10 μM). Fig. 4A shows that the sensor array successfully distinguished the five mixtures of 2-NP and 3-NP at different ratios, and the two factors exhibit good clustering properties with a total variance dispersion of 98.93%. All 25 cases (5 NPs mixtures with 5 replicates) were exactly assigned to their individual groups (Fig. 4B). Similarly, the mixtures of 2-NP/4-NP, 3-NP/4-NP and 2-NP/3-NP/4-NP were well discriminated, and two-dimensional maps were obtained (Figs. S9–11). At the same time, interference from other phenolic substances to NPs discrimination was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4C, all interfering substances are separated well from the three clusters of NPs, implying remarkable anti-interference ability of the sensor arrays. Repeatability of the μPAD was evaluated through repetitive measurements of the analytes, which yielded relative standard deviations (RSDs) less than 1.5% (n = 20) (Fig. S12A). For long-term stability test, the sensing responses demonstrated no obvious decrease after 15 days (Fig. S12B). These results demonstrate the high feasibility of this device to identify NPs in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of blind and real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the practicality of the μPAD, single NP isomers, and their binary and ternary mixtures (total concentration of 10 μM) were used as blind samples. As shown in Table S1, 54 blind samples were identified exactly. To further explore the application of the device in real samples, water samples from river, tap and lake spiked with NPs were determined. As shown in Figs. 4D and S13, three NPs were well identified at the concentration of 10 μM, indicating that the platform can be used for real-sample analysis.

In summary, an inexpensive, portable, and easy-to-use fluorescent sensor array platform was designed to solve the difficulty in identifying chemical species with similar structure. The recognition principle of the sensor array was based on the specific response of MIPs to templates and the cross-reactivity of MIPs towards template analogs. To verify the feasibility of the approach, three NP isomers, all being the common environmental contaminants, were distinguished as model analytes. LDA and HCA results determined that the platform was powerful for discrimination of single and mixed NP isomers with 100% accuracy. In addition, the successful discrimination and determination of real and blind samples further validated its practical application. Thus, the developed MIPs/CQDs/Paper can be considered as an effective tool for environmental contaminants monitoring and extended for identification of other analytes by alternating template molecules. The platform can be further improved in the aspect of portability by equipping a miniaturized fluorometer, which enables implementation of on-site monitoring work.

Nitrophenols (NPs), as important industrial raw materials, have caused serious pollution to the environment and been classified as priority toxic pollutants and hazardous wastes in many countries. We present a facile way to prepare a novel fluorescence sensor-array μPAD for discrimination of three NP isomers. Each sensing unit on the array showed differential yet cross-reactive binding affinity to NP isomers, resulting in distinct fluorescence quenching efficiency. The platform exhibited excellent performance in discrimination and determination of single and mixed NP isomers with 100% accuracy.

Liang Zhu: Methodology, Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Xuecui Mei: Methodology, Investigation. Zhengchun Peng: Methodology, Investigation. Jiao Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Yingchun Li: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work financially supported by Shenzhen Science and Technology Program (KQTD20170810105439418), National Natural Science Foundation of China (82104121, 81973280, 82173778), and Key Program of Natural Science Foundation of Shenzhen (JCYJ20200109113410174).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2022.129012.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Bai et al., 2020

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual-emission carbon dots-stabilized copper nanoclusters for ratiometric and visual detection of Cr2O7 2- ions and Cd2+ ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Bai, H., Tu, Z., Liu, Y., Tai, Q., Guo, Z., Liu, S., 2020. Dual-emission carbon dots-stabilized copper nanoclusters for ratiometric and visual detection of Cr2O72- ions and Cd2+ ions. J. Hazard. Mater. 386, 121654.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Cate et al., 2015

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent developments in paper-based microfluidic devices

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Cate, D.M., Adkins, J.A., Mettakoonpitak, J., Henry, C.S., 2015. Recent developments in paper-based microfluidic devices. Anal. chem. 87, 19–41.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Chi et al., 2013

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bioaugmentation with a consortium of bacterial nitrophenol-degraders for remediation of soil contaminated with three nitrophenol isomers
